name: 있어요 vs 없어요 — Have / Don't Have

levels.grammarNote: Possession in Korean: [thing]이/가 있어요. 돈 ends in consonant (n), so 이. Literally: 'Money exists.'
시간 ends in consonant (n), so 이. 없어요 = doesn't exist / don't have.
질문 ends in consonant (n), so 이. Rising intonation makes it a question.
친구 ends in vowel (u), so 가. 한국 친구 = Korean friend(s).
Existence: [place]에 [thing]이/가 있어요. 화장실 ends in ㄹ (consonant), so 이.

levels.front: There is no milk in the refrigerator.
levels.grammarNote: 우유 ends in vowel (u), so 가. 없어요 negates the existence.
Progressive tense: verb stem + 고 있어요. 있어요 here signals ongoing action, not possession.
공부하다 (study) → 공부하고 있어요 (am studying). 고 있어요 = progressive.
Formal polite form of 있다. Used in official, news, or very formal contexts. 있어요 is sufficient for most daily situations.
Formal polite form of 없다. Paired with 있습니다 in formal registers.
In casual speech the subject marker can be dropped. 물 있어요? is a natural, short question. Full form: 물이 있어요?
사람 ends in consonant (m), so 이. 많이 = a lot, many.
약속 ends in consonant (k), so 이. Very common phrase to explain you're busy.
돈 ends in consonant (n), so 이. 없어요 = don't have / doesn't exist.
Casual form — used with close friends. Drop 요 from 있어요 → 있어.
Casual form of 없어요. Drop 요 → 없어. Only with close friends of similar age.
